confidence.disclosure: Reconstructed from the Gazavat-i Hayreddin Pasha (the memoirs Barbarossa dictated to Muradi Sinan Reis), Ottoman chronicles, and the Spanish, Venetian and French archival record. Most major campaigns are documented to the month or day. Ernle Bradford's The Sultan's Admiral provides the modern narrative spine.

sources: Muradi Sinan Reis, Gazavat-i Hayreddin Pasha (c. 1545)
Ernle Bradford, The Sultan's Admiral: The Life of Barbarossa (1968)
Emrah Safa Gürkan, Sultanın Korsanları: Osmanlı Akdenizi'nde Gazâ, Yağma ve Esaret 1500-1700 (2018)

journey.event: Born Hizir on the recently Ottoman island of Lesbos to a sipahi father and a Greek mother.
Joined his elder brother Oruç in establishing a corsair base on the island of Djerba under the Hafsids of Tunis.
Took part in his brother's failed assault on the Spanish-held fortress of Bougie, where Oruç lost his left arm.
Joined the corsair raid on Valencia that captured four Spanish ships.
Captured Algiers from the Spanish protectorate; Oruç declared himself sultan of the city.
Inherited the leadership of Algiers after Oruç was killed in the Spanish-led siege of Tlemcen.
Offered Algiers to Sultan Selim I in exchange for Ottoman troops, becoming Beylerbey of the new Ottoman regency.
Captured the Peñón of Algiers, the small Spanish island fortress in the harbour, removing the last Habsburg foothold in the city.
Raided the Knights of St John at Tripoli, which had been granted to them by Charles V the previous year.
Raided Bonifacio, Montecristo, Elba and Lampedusa during his journey east to answer Suleiman's summons.
Received by Suleiman the Magnificent at Topkapı Palace and appointed Kapudan Pasha (Grand Admiral) of the Ottoman Navy.
Sacked Reggio Calabria and a string of Italian coastal towns at the start of the 1534 campaign.
Captured Tunis and the fortress of La Goulette on 16 August 1534, expelling the Hafsid sultan Mulay Hasan.
Lost Tunis to Charles V's expedition; escaped overland with his fleet from Bône (Annaba).
Sacked Mahón on the Balearic island of Menorca, taking some 6,000 captives in retaliation for the loss of Tunis.
Raided the Apulian coast in support of Suleiman's campaign in southern Italy.
Took part in the unsuccessful Ottoman siege of Venetian Corfu.
Won a decisive victory over Andrea Doria's Holy League fleet at the Battle of Preveza on 28 September 1538, securing Ottoman naval dominance in the eastern Mediterranean.
Captured Venetian outposts in the Adriatic during the closing phase of the Ottoman-Venetian war.
Received with full honours at Marseille on 23 July 1543 as part of the Franco-Ottoman alliance against Charles V.
Joint Franco-Ottoman force besieged and took Nice on 22 August 1543.
Wintered the Ottoman fleet at Toulon for eight months at Francis I's invitation, with the city's cathedral converted to a mosque.
Threatened Genoa to force the release of his lieutenant Turgut Reis from Christian captivity.
Retired to his seaside palace at Büyükdere on the Bosphorus, dictating his memoirs to Muradi Sinan Reis.
Died on 4 July 1546 in Istanbul; buried in the mausoleum at Beşiktaş designed by Mimar Sinan.
